region_of_origin: Global (widely produced and consumed in Singapore and internationally)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for raw processed chicken ham; closest defensible parent anchor is 'chicken meat food product' (FOODON_03302349). Exact term for cured/processed chicken ham not available in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for raw processed chicken ham; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of chicken ham is approximately 2 slices (50g), commonly used in sandwiches, wraps, or as a protein component in a meal.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Ham — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, dietary fibre) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chicken ham, raw (F11070002). Sodium value estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for chicken ham (NDB No. 07028, SR Legacy) as HPB does not publish sodium for this entry; value cross-referenced with typical cured chicken ham sodium range of 900–1200 mg/100g. Saturated fat and sugar estimated from USDA SR Legacy closest match. Secondary micronutrients set to -1 where not reported by either source.

gi_source.title: N/A — Glycemic index not applicable for negligible-carbohydrate processed meat
gi_source.source_match_notes: Chicken ham, raw contains 0g carbohydrate per 100g. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. No GI value assigned per Project Veritas v2 rules for zero-carbohydrate foods. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (~1100 mg/100g) from curing process, exceeding the 600 mg/100g threshold; sodium nitrates used in curing further elevate vascular risk.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit chicken ham due to its very high sodium content (~1100 mg/100g); if consumed, restrict to small portions and pair with high-potassium foods such as banana or spinach to partially offset the sodium load, and always check product labels as sodium varies by brand.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0g/100g) means minimal acute glycaemic impact, but regular consumption of processed cured meats is associated with increased insulin resistance and elevated cardiovascular risk in people with Type 2 Diabetes.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken ham has no direct glycaemic impact due to zero carbohydrate content, but people with Type 2 Diabetes should limit processed meat frequency per ADA cardiovascular risk guidelines and prefer unprocessed chicken breast as a lower-risk high-protein alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (~1100 mg/100g), saturated fat (~2.45g/100g), and sodium nitrates from curing are all established cardiovascular risk factors; processed meat consumption is independently associated with increased CVD mortality in epidemiological studies.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should minimise consumption of chicken ham due to its high sodium, saturated fat, and nitrate content; unprocessed lean poultry is strongly preferred as a heart-healthy protein source.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium nitrates and high sodium in cured processed meats promote systemic inflammation and may worsen RA-related joint swelling and inflammatory markers.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid processed cured meats such as chicken ham and substitute with plain grilled or poached chicken to reduce pro-inflammatory nitrate and sodium exposure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken ham (raw) is a processed, cured poultry product made primarily from chicken meat that has been shaped, seasoned, and preserved using salt, sodium nitrate, phosphates, and water to mimic the texture and appearance of traditional pork ham. In its raw (uncooked) state, it is typically sold as a chilled or vacuum-packed block or roll, ready to be sliced and cooked or consumed after heating. The texture is firm and dense, with a mild, slightly salty flavour characteristic of cured meats. It is widely available in Singapore supermarkets and is commonly used in sandwiches, fried rice, pasta, and as a protein component in school canteen and hawker-style meals. Nutritionally, chicken ham is a high-protein food (approximately 28g protein per 100g) with moderate fat (8.6g/100g) and negligible carbohydrate (0g/100g), making it keto-compatible in macronutrient terms. However, its very high sodium content (~1100 mg/100g) makes it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The curing agents (sodium nitrates) used in production are associated with increased cardiovascular and inflammatory risk. Despite its protein density, health authorities including the ADA and WHO recommend limiting processed meat consumption due to long-term metabolic and cardiovascular risks. It is classified as a processed food under NOVA classification level 3.

Is chicken ham high in protein?
What is the difference between chicken ham and regular ham?
Can I eat chicken ham if I have high blood pressure?
How many calories in chicken ham?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (F11070002, confidence 0.95). Sodium estimated from USDA SR Legacy closest match for chicken ham (NDB 07028) and cross-validated against typical cured chicken ham sodium range; documented as adapted. Saturated fat and sugar estimated from USDA SR Legacy. All values are internally consistent and plausible for a processed cured chicken product.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database entry F11070002 — chicken ham is a protein-and-fat product with no added carbohydrate by formulation; confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero as this is a processed meat product containing no plant-derived fibre; confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo. Sugar_g is confirmed zero by formulation — no sugars added in standard chicken ham production; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy analogous entry. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0g/100g); GI set to null and gi_category set to not_applicable per Project Veritas v2 rules for zero-carbohydrate foods. Sodium value (~1100 mg/100g) is an adapted estimate from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 07028, chicken ham) as HPB Foodo does not publish sodium for this entry; actual sodium varies significantly by brand and product formulation — consumers should check product labels. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for processed chicken ham not fully reported in HPB Foodo F11070002 or USDA SR Legacy NDB 07028 as of 2026. FoodOn match type set to closest_parent — no exact FoodOn term for raw processed chicken ham exists; FOODON_03302349 (chicken meat food product) used as closest defensible parent anchor. Local-language names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) added to reflect Singapore market context where this product is widely consumed and labelled in multiple languages. This record is classified as a generic processed poultry product; individual branded products may differ significantly in sodium, fat, and additive content.
